perturbation.original_text: An employee, director, officer, manager, member, partner, affiliate, associate, agent, attorney, accountant, consultant, banker, business adviser, financial adviser, scientific adviser or technical adviser of Receiver may become a party to this Agreement by si gning a counterpart hereof, a copy of which shall be provided to VIDAR wit hin five days of signature.
perturbation.changed_text: An employee, director, officer, manager, member, partner, affiliate, associate, agent, attorney, accountant, consultant, banker, business adviser, financial adviser, scientific adviser or technical adviser of Receiver may become a party to this Agreement by signing a counterpart hereof, a copy of which shall be provided to VIDAR within thirty days of signature.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification period to 30 days introduces a possible conflict with Virginia's trade secret law, which mandates reasonable steps to protect trade secrets. Delaying notification could be deemed unreasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia Code § 59.1-336 states that to be eligible for protection under trade secret law, the owner must take measures that are reasonable under the circumstances to protect the trade secret. A 30-day delay in notifying the original party of additional receivers may not be viewed as reasonable, leading to loss of protection for disclosed secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 59.1-336. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-336 . Short title and definitions. As used in this chapter, which may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, use of a computer or computer network without authority, breach of a duty or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: 1. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or 2. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (1) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (2) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (4) Acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The modification from five to thirty days for providing a copy of the agreement introduces a potential conflict with Virginia trade secret law. According to the LLM explanation and perturbation explanation, Virginia Code § 59.1-336 requires 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets. Delaying notification could be deemed unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation', implying a need for timely action to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of trade secrets. The changed text's longer notification period weakens the ability to take prompt action, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under the law.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the request of VIDAR or the termination or expiration of this Confidential Agreement, Re ceiver shall promptly return to VIDAR all copies of the Confidential Information and obtained by Receiver.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the request of VIDAR or the termination or expiration of this Confidential Agreement, Receiver shall return to VIDAR all copies of the Confidential Information obtained by Receiver within 60 days.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly' to a 60-day window might be unreasonable and conflict with the duty to protect trade secrets under Virginia law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia Code § 59.1-336 requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. A 60-day delay in returning confidential information after a request or termination could be deemed an unreasonable delay, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'promptly' to 'within 60 days' introduces an inconsistency regarding the time-sensitive obligation to return confidential information. The Law Explanation argues that a 60-day delay could jeopardize trade secret protection, which aligns with the Perturbation Explanation's concern about conflicting with the duty to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' and mentions the duty to maintain secrecy, suggesting that a delayed return could potentially lead to misappropriation or breach of that duty. Therefore, the modified text introduces a contradiction by potentially undermining the required level of protection for trade secrets under Virginia law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement becomes effectiv e on the Effective Date and continues in effect thereafter for so long as any Conf idential Information continues to exist.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement becomes effective on the Effective Date and continues in effect for a period of two years, regardless of whether the Confidential Information continues to exist.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the agreement to two years, even if confidential information still exists, contradicts the premise of trade secret protection under Virginia law, which can last indefinitely as long as the information remains a secret and reasonable measures are taken to protect it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia Code § 59.1-336 implies indefinite protection for trade secrets as long as they remain secret and reasonable steps are taken to maintain their secrecy. Limiting the NDA to two years, regardless of whether the information remains confidential, undermines the potential for long-term trade secret protection.

"Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the agreement to two years, even if confidential information still exists. This contradicts the implied indefinite protection for trade secrets under Virginia law, as explained in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' and discusses duties to maintain secrecy, suggesting an expectation of ongoing protection as long as the information remains a trade secret, which the two-year limit undermines. The original contract's indefinite duration based on the existence of Confidential Information aligns with the idea of ongoing protection, while the changed text introduces a contradiction by creating a fixed termination date regardless of the confidential information's status.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be gov erned by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and all actions involving this Agreement shall be brought in the state or federal courts located in the Commonwealth of Virginia, to which the parties consent to jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California, and all actions involving this agreement shall be resolved through mandatory arbitration in Los Angeles, California, waiving the right to a jury trial.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and dispute resolution to mandatory arbitration in California could potentially conflict with Virginia law, which allows for litigation in Virginia courts. This is especially problematic if the contract involves trade secrets which are protected under Virginia law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-335
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia Code § 59.1-335 provides for civil actions in Virginia courts for misappropriation of trade secrets. Forcing arbitration in California and applying California law could potentially limit the remedies available under Virginia's trade secret law and may not adequately protect VIDAR's trade secrets, given the agreement was established in Virginia.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 25. Virginia Membership Camping Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-335 . Penalties. A. Any person violating any of the provisions of §§ 59.1-317 , 59.1-318 , 59.1-326 , 59.1-327 , 59.1-328 , and 59.1-329 or a cease and desist or temporary order issued pursuant to § 59.1-324 shall be guilty of a Class 1 misdemeanor and any person violating any of the provisions of § 59.1-332 shall be guilty of a Class 3 misdemeanor. Other than with reference to § 59.1-332 each violation shall be deemed a separate offense. B. Any violation of the provisions of this chapter shall constitute a prohibited practice pursuant to the provisions of § 59.1-200 and shall be subject to any and all of the enforcement provisions of the Virginia Consumer Protection Act (§ 59.1-196 et seq.) of this title. 1985, c. 409; 1992, c. 545. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Virginia law and courts for dispute resolution, while the changed text mandates California law and arbitration in Los Angeles. The Law Explanation states this shift could limit remedies available under Virginia's trade secret law (Virginia Code § 59.1-335). Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing § 59.1-335, confirms Virginia provides for penalties and civil actions related to violations, suggesting a potential contradiction if these are bypassed by the California arbitration clause.
